claim: Isothermal constant strain rate compression tests were performed at temperatures of 900, 950, 1000, 1050, 1100, and 1150 °C and strain rates of 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 10 s⁻¹, with a maximum true strain of 0.92 (60% reduction).
evidence_text: The experimental temperatures were 900, 950, 1000, 1050, 1100 and 1150 °C, and the strain rates were 0.01, 0.1, 1 and 10 s⁻¹, respectively. The maximum compression was 60% (corresponding to the maximum true strain 0.92).

claim: The sample was heated to 1200 °C at 10 °C/s, homogenized by holding for 120 s, then cooled at 5 °C/s to the deformation temperature, held for 15 s to equilibrate temperature, and compressed, followed by water quenching.
evidence_text: The sample was heated to 1200 °C at a rate of 10 °C/s, then homogenized by holding for 120 s, and finally cooled to the set T at a rate of 5 °C/s. The sample was for 15 s at the set T before compression, so the temperature difference between the inside and outside of the sample was the same. After compression, the sample was sprayed with water to cool the steel down.

claim: Cylindrical samples of diameter 8 mm and height 12 mm were used, with mica foils containing high-temperature lubricant affixed to both end faces to reduce friction and non-uniformity of deformation.
evidence_text: The forging billet was processed into a cylindrical sample of F 8 mm 12 mm, and the perpendicularity of both ends of the samples was ensured. The mica foils with high-temperature lubricants were affixed to the two end faces of the sample to reduce the friction and the non-uniformity of deformation.

claim: The dynamic recrystallization activation energy of the Fe-Mn-Al-C steel is 669.844 kJ mol⁻¹.
evidence_text: The dynamic recrystallization activation energy is 669.844 kJ mol⁻¹.

claim: With the decrease of strain rate or the increase of temperature, the Zener-Hollomon parameter decreases gradually, dynamic recrystallization behavior is more likely to occur, and the grain size increases.
evidence_text: With the decrease of ε or the increase of T, the Z parameter decreases gradually and the dynamic recrystallization behavior is more likely to occur; however, its grain size increases accordingly.

claim: Fine and uniform grain structure can be obtained by hot working in the range of 950–1000 °C / 0.01–0.1 s⁻¹ and 1050–1100 °C / 1–10 s⁻¹.
evidence_text: The fine and uniform grain structure can be obtained by hot working in the range of 950–1000 °C/0.01–0.1 s⁻¹ and 1050–1100 °C/1–10 s⁻¹.

claim: The flow stress of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel decreases with decreasing strain rate or increasing temperature, exhibiting negative temperature sensitivity and positive strain-rate sensitivity.
evidence_text: The decrease of ε_ or the increase of T will result in the decrease of the rheological stress, indicating that the steel is a negative temperature-sensitive and positive strain rate-sensitive material.

claim: At higher temperatures, accelerated grain boundary mobility strengthens DRX softening and reduces dislocation slip resistance; at lower strain rates, more time is available for DRX and DRV, causing the softening effect to exceed work hardening and reduce flow stress.
evidence_text: The former is primarily due to the accelerated grain boundary mobility of the dynamic recrystallization at a higher T. Furthermore, high temperatures are beneficial in strengthening the softening effect and reducing the dislocation slip resistance. The latter is primarily due to the lower ε_ providing more time to promote DRX and dynamic recovery (DRV). This leads to the work hardening effect being less than the softening effect of the material, showing that the rheological stress decreases with the decrease of ε_.

claim: At a constant strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹, the peak stress dropped from 305.4 MPa at 900 °C to 53.8 MPa at 1150 °C, corresponding to an 82% decrease.
evidence_text: At the low ε_ (0.01 s⁻¹), the peak stress decreased from 305.4 MPa at 900 °C to 53.8 MPa at 1150 °C, a reduction of 82%.

claim: At a strain rate of 10 s⁻¹, the peak stress decreased by 73% over the temperature range from 900 to 1150 °C.
evidence_text: When the ε_ is high (10 s⁻¹), the peak stress decreases by 73% within the same temperature range

claim: The greater reduction in peak stress at the lower strain rate (82% at 0.01 s⁻¹ vs. 73% at 10 s⁻¹) indicates that strain rate has a significant influence on peak stress.
evidence_text: When the ε_ is high (10 s⁻¹), the peak stress decreases by 73% within the same temperature range, indicating that ε_ has a greater influence on the peak stress.

claim: At a constant strain rate, the peak stress decreases with increasing temperature, indicating that peak stress is more sensitive to temperature under constant strain rate conditions.
evidence_text: in the case of a constant ε_, the higher the T, the lower the peak stress, indicating that the peak stress is more sensitive to T.

claim: Increasing temperature raises thermal activation energy, accelerates grain boundary mobility, improves recrystallization, lowers dislocation slip resistance, and strengthens the softening effect, thus reducing peak stress.
evidence_text: This is because the higher the T, the higher the thermal activation energy of the material, the faster the grain boundary mobility, the better the recrystallization and the lower the slip resistance, the stronger the softening effect and the lower the peak stress.

claim: Using the Arrhenius hyperbolic sine equation, the DRX activation energy Qp is determined to be 669.844 kJ mol⁻¹, based on linear fitting slopes k1=5.135 (ln[sinh(ασp)] vs lnε̇) and k2=15.690 (ln[sinh(ασp)] vs 1000/T).
evidence_text: the average slopes of the curves in Fig. 4 (a) and (b) are k1=5.135 and k2=15.690, respectively. Furthermore, the dynamic recrystallization activation energy of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel is 669.844 kJ mol⁻¹ according to formula (2)

claim: The stress level factor α in the Arrhenius sinh equation is α = β/n1 = 0.00599.
evidence_text: the stress level factor a is determined by the two constants (n1 and b), that is α = β/n1 = (d ln ε̇ / d σ) / (d ln ε̇ / d ln σ) = 0.00599.

claim: The Zener-Hollomon parameter Z increases with increasing strain rate or decreasing temperature, mirroring the trend of the flow stress.
evidence_text: Table 1 shows that the Z parameter increases with the increase of ε_ or the decrease of T, which is the same as that of the flow stress.

claim: Fig. 3(a) and Fig. 3(b) present the rheological flow stress curves of the Fe-Mn-Al-C steel at temperatures from 900 to 1150 °C and strain rates from 0.01 to 10 s⁻¹.
evidence_text: Fig. 3 (a) and (b) show the rheological stress curves of FeeMneAleC steel at different T (900e1150 -C) and ε_ (0.01e10 s⁻¹).

claim: Fig. 3(c) and Fig. 3(d) show 3D maps of peak stress as a function of temperature and strain rate for the Fe-Mn-Al-C steel.
evidence_text: Fig. 3 (c) and (d) are 3D peak stress maps of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel under different T and ε_.

claim: Fig. 4 displays the linear relationship maps of lnε̇ vs ln[sinh(ασp)] and ln[sinh(ασp)] vs 1000/T used to determine the DRX activation energy.
evidence_text: Fig. 4 e Relationship maps of lnε_ ln½sinhðaspÞ (a) and ln½sinhðaspÞ 1000=T (b).

claim: The Z parameter increases with increasing strain rate or decreasing deformation temperature, mirroring the flow stress trend.
evidence_text: Table 1 shows that the Z parameter increases with the increase of ε_ or the decrease of T, which is the same as that of the flow stress.

claim: The ratio of critical strain to peak strain for dynamic recrystallization is determined to be εc/εp ≈ 0.452 using the Cingara-McQueen equation and inflection point analysis.
evidence_text: The functional relationship between the required εc and the εp is εc/εp ≈ 0.452.

claim: Multiple linear regression yields power-law relationships between characteristic flow curve values (εc, σc, εp, σp, εs, σs) and lnZ, with quadratic models for stresses and linear models for strains.
evidence_text: The Z parameter in Table 1 is introduced to characterize the functional relationship between the characteristic values of the rheological stress curves of FeeMneAleC steel, as shown in Fig. 6. The mathematical models of εc/σc, εp/σp and εs/σs of the dynamic recrystallization are obtained by multiple linear regression fittings:

claim: A DRX state diagram (Fig. 7) divides hot deformation conditions into work hardening (area A), partial DRX (area B), and complete DRX (area C), with critical and steady-state strains increasing with Z parameter.
evidence_text: the dynamic recrystallization state diagram of hot deformation of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel can be drawn, as shown in Fig. 7, in which area A is the zone of work hardening, area B is the zone of partial DRX and area C is the zone of complete DRX. As it can be seen from Fig. 7, with the increase of the Z parameter, that is, with the increase of ε_ or the decrease of T, the εc and εs of dynamic recrystallization increases.

claim: At 1100 °C and a strain rate of 10 s⁻¹, the large deformation zone exhibits serrated grain boundaries and small equiaxed necklace-like DRX grains along elongated original boundaries; decreasing strain rate leads to grain growth and improved microstructural homogeneity.
evidence_text: the grain boundaries of the large deformation zone were serrated at ε = 10 s⁻¹. The DRX grains are distributed along the elongated original grain boundary in the form of a small and equiaxed necklace. With the decrease of ε, the recrystallized grains grew and the degree of microstructure heterogeneity improved (Fig. 9 (a) and (b)).

claim: Increasing temperature strongly promotes DRX: at 1050 °C and 0.1 s⁻¹ DRX is essentially complete in the large deformation zone, and recrystallized grain size grows markedly with further temperature rise.
evidence_text: At 1050 °C, the dynamic recrystallization is basically completed within the large deformation zone, and the recrystallized grains size grows significantly with the increase of T.

claim: The average dynamically recrystallized grain size increases with decreasing strain rate or increasing temperature; at 1150 °C and 0.01 s⁻¹ the grain size exceeds 70 μm, indicating coarse grains and rapid growth.
evidence_text: the DRX grain size of the steel after hot deformation increases with the decrease of ε_ or the increase of T. Among them, when the T is 1150 °C and the ε_ is 0.01 s⁻¹, the grain size exceeds 70 μm, and the structure is coarse grain, indicating that the recrystallized grains under this condition have grown rapidly.

claim: Sufficient DRX with relatively fine and uniform microstructure is achieved in the processing windows 950–1000 °C / 0.01–0.1 s⁻¹ and 1050–1100 °C / 1–10 s⁻¹.
evidence_text: sufficient dynamic recrystallization can be obtained by hot working within the range of 950-1000 °C / 0.01-0.1 s⁻¹ and 1050-1100 °C / 1-10 s⁻¹, and the microstructure of the grain is relatively fine and uniform.

claim: The peak stress of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel during hot deformation can be reduced by decreasing the strain rate or increasing the deformation temperature.
evidence_text: During hot deformation, the peak stress of the steel can be reduced by decreasing ε_ or increasing T.

claim: The dynamic recrystallization activation energy of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel is 669.844 kJ mol⁻¹, calculated using the Arrhenius hyperbolic sine function.
evidence_text: based on the Arrhenius hyperbolic sine function equation, the dynamic recrystallization activation energy of the steel was calculated as 669.844 kJ mol⁻¹

claim: Fine and uniform dynamic recrystallization grains can be obtained by hot working in the ranges 950–1000 °C / 0.01–0.1 s⁻¹ and 1050–1100 °C / 1–10 s⁻¹.
evidence_text: the fine and uniform grain structure can be obtained by hot working in the range of 950e1000 -C/0.01e0.1 s 1 and 1050e1100 -C/1e10 s 1 .

claim: The prediction model for dynamic recrystallization average grain size is d_rex = 807743.63 ε̇⁻°·¹⁹⁰³ exp(−120648.47/RT) + 4.338, with deviation generally lower than 3 μm and mean relative error 4.53%.
evidence_text: The prediction model of dynamic recrystallization average grain size is drex ¼ 807743:63ε_0:1903 expð 120648:47 =RTÞ þ 4:338, and the deviation is generally lower than 3 μm , and MRE for all samples is only 4.53%.
